mnemonics.title: LSTM Gates Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: FIDO: Forget (F), Input (I), Dark (output/Decide), Output (O) - FIDO remembers what to FORGET, what to INPUT, and what to OUTPUT/DECIDE

mnemonics.title: GRU Simpler Gates
mnemonics.content: GRU = Update (combines forget+input) + Reset - GRU has RESET to forget irrelevant past and UPDATE to decide new memory

mnemonics.title: BPTT Steps
mnemonics.content: UNROLL: Unroll network → Forward pass → Compute loss → Backward pass through time → Update weights

mnemonics.title: RNN Problem Solutions
mnemonics.content: GIVE: Gating (LSTM/GRU) solves vanishing, Initialization helps, Variance scaling, Exploding = clip gradients

acronyms.usage: Neural network with loops to process sequential data
RNN variant with gating to capture long-term dependencies
Training algorithm for RNNs by unrolling through time
Architecture mapping input to output sequence of different length
Tagging words with grammatical categories using many-to-many RNN

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a time dimension as a chain - each link is a copy of the same network processing one element, with arrows connecting hidden states across time showing information flow
Think of LSTM as a conveyor belt (cell state) with three guards (gates) controlling what gets on, what gets off, and what passes through
Picture gradient as water flowing backward through a leaky pipe - by the time it reaches distant past, it's just a trickle (gradient approaches zero)
Imagine a spotlight that dynamically focuses on different parts of input while producing each output - the spotlight position changes based on relevance

analogies.analogy: Like a human reading a book - your understanding at each point depends on what you just read plus everything you remember from before
Like a email filter: Forget gate deletes old emails, Input gate decides which new ones to keep, Output gate selects what to show
Like rewinding a video to understand how each scene contributed to the final plot - trace consequences backward through time
Like trying to translate a long paragraph by reading it once and memorizing everything - you'll forget important details by the end
Like reading a mystery novel - you understand the present better when you know both what happened before AND what happens later (foreshadowing)
